quote: Oh, okay. I've I've got it here for a 1320 Greta Boulevard.
stewart_read: Rocco denies submitting a form. Meg's response is to read back the address rather than acknowledge the confusion and re-establish legitimacy. The codex's standard intro requires referencing the form submission to legitimize the call — when the customer denies the form, the legitimacy anchor is gone and needs to be rebuilt before anything else. Meg skips the rebuild entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg reads the address to confirm she has the right person before proceeding. Rejected — there's no follow-up legitimacy move after the address confirmation, so this reads as procedural rather than strategic.
coaching_implication: When a customer denies the form, the rep needs a one-line legitimacy recovery before moving to pitch. Suggested rephrase intent: 'I may have the wrong person — I do have your address on file, so let me just confirm I'm reaching the right household before I take another second of your time.' This keeps the door open without arguing about the form.

stewart_read: Rocco's 'I don't want any of those' at 00:51 is a cold first-30-seconds refusal — the hello_objection protocol applies. The correct move is acknowledge_then_small_ask: validate that he doesn't know Meg from anyone, then ask for 30 seconds to explain why she's calling. Instead, Meg asks 'any specific reason why?' — an open-ended probe that invites Rocco to build his case against solar, which he does at length. This is not the changed_mind rebuttal scenario (which presupposes prior interest); it's a cold gate that needed a small-ask, not an interrogation.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg probes for the objection to understand it before rebutting. Rejected — the probe produces a neighbor anecdote that Meg then argues against for 90 seconds, making the probe counterproductive.
coaching_implication: Walk Meg through protocols.hello_objection_not_interested. The 30-second small-ask is the correct move here. Play the call from 00:51 and ask her: 'What would you have said instead of asking him why?' The goal is to get her to generate the small-ask language herself.

quote: It our neighbor on the street has it. And, you don't pay how much for the electric, but still he's paying for the for the solar energy panels and everything like that. He's losing money.
stewart_read: Rocco's neighbor anecdote is a concrete, lived objection — not an abstract concern. The high-leverage move here is to validate the observation ('That's a fair thing to notice') and then redirect to Rocco's specific situation rather than arguing about the neighbor's system. Meg counters directly ('No. Not at all.'), which signals to Rocco that his real-world experience is being dismissed. The objection_inversion play — 'What your neighbor is paying for is the gap between what his system covers and what the utility charges for the rest; the design we'd build for you is sized to your usage' — would have acknowledged the concern while opening curiosity. No fabricated numbers needed; the move is structural.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Meg is correcting a factual misunderstanding about how solar billing works. Partially true, but the correction without validation entrenches rather than opens. The strategic read (validate + redirect) is more supported by the subsequent escalation.
coaching_implication: This is the call's highest-leverage missed moment. Show Meg the three-touch pattern (01:09, 01:38, 02:19) and ask: 'At what point did arguing stop working?' Then introduce the validate-and-redirect structure. The neighbor anecdote needed one acknowledgment, not three rebuttals.

quote: I mean, but I'm still paying for the panels and the solar energy if it's not my house.
stewart_read: The phrase 'if it's not my house' introduces potential homeownership ambiguity. Meg never confirms whether Rocco owns the property at 1320 Greta Boulevard. The codex marks address_homeowner_confirmed as hard-required with hard_dq_if_missing: not_homeowner. If Rocco is a renter, the call was already a hard DQ on a separate dimension before the utility question was ever asked. Meg does not probe this — she continues the feature debate. The ambiguity is unresolved at call end.
alternative_hypothesis: The phrase 'if it's not my house' may be a hypothetical construction ('even if it weren't my house, I'd still be paying') rather than a literal ownership disclosure. Possible — but the codex's hard-required homeownership confirm means the rep should have clarified regardless of interpretation.
coaching_implication: Flag for the manager: we don't know if Rocco owns the home. The verify beat (homeownership confirm) was never run. On future calls, Meg should run address_homeowner_confirmed within the first 60 seconds — before any pitch — so a renter DQ surfaces immediately rather than after 90 seconds of debate.

quote: They got panels all over the roof there and everything. And he still pays electric bill.
stewart_read: This is the third touch of the neighbor objection. Meg's response — 'Do you know if the solar is covering all of his electric needs or just parts?' — is a clarifying question, not a redirect. At this point the objection has been raised three times and Meg has argued against it each time. The codex's multi_angle_resilience principle calls for 3+ angles per objection before giving up — but the angles should be different approaches, not the same counter-argument restated. Meg has used one angle (feature explanation) three times.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Meg's clarifying question is an attempt to find a crack in the neighbor anecdote. Rejected — the question doesn't lead anywhere; Rocco responds with a final dismissal at 02:33.
coaching_implication: Use this as a multi_angle_resilience teaching moment. Meg ran one angle three times. Show her what a second angle looks like: moving from feature explanation to curiosity creation ('I'd be curious what his bill looks like now vs before — do you know?') or to a verify pivot ('Let me ask you something different — what does your own bill run?').

quote: Let me ask you I think my phone is gonna work. Are you with the Valley Electric? Is that your power provider?
stewart_read: The utility company question — verify beat order 4 — arrives at 02:38, after 90 seconds of feature debate. Of the 8 required verify items, Meg completes at most 2 (utility company and approximate bill amount, both surfaced at 02:46). The codex requires 6-of-8 for verify completion. Running utility first would have either DQ'd the call at 00:38 (saving 2 minutes) or opened a productive path by anchoring to Rocco's actual situation before pitching. The call_shape beat sequence exists precisely to prevent this — verify before pitch, not after.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Meg delayed verify because she was trying to overcome the objection first. This is the failure mode — the pitch without verify context gave Rocco nothing to anchor to except his neighbor's experience.
coaching_implication: This is the structural coaching point for this call. Meg ran the call in the wrong order: pitch → objection loop → verify. The correct sequence is intro → verify → pitch. Ask Meg: 'If you'd asked about his utility company at 00:38, what's the earliest this call could have ended?' The answer is 90 seconds. That's the cost of the sequencing error.

quote: I actually know that we we don't work with Valley Electric anyway. So I'll just take you off my list here. I appreciate your time.
stewart_read: The DQ exit is procedurally correct — Ion doesn't serve Valley Electric customers, and Meg exits cleanly without arguing. Credit for the clean exit. However, the utility question arrived 2+ minutes after it should have, after a feature debate that entrenched Rocco's resistance and produced no usable qualification data. The exit is right; the timing is the coaching gap.
coaching_implication: Acknowledge the clean exit to Meg — she didn't drag it out once the DQ was confirmed. Then pivot to the sequencing question: 'What if you'd asked about the utility company right after he said he didn't know about the form?' The goal is to get her to see that verify-first protects her time on every call, not just this one.
